source_paper_title: Specific Wasserstein Divergence Between Continuous Martingales
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Backhoff_Veraguas_2026, title={Specific Wasserstein Divergence Between Continuous Martingales}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2025.0896}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2025.0896}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Backhoff-Veraguas, Julio and Zhang, Xin}, year={2026}, month=jan }
context:
Page 8, Remark 2.4 (end of the remark, following equation (2.1))

We consider the scaling limit of (2.1) for our choice \(\alpha_1(\cdot)=\pm W_1(\cdot, P)^p\) as an interesting open question which we do not tackle here. Our main result (Theorem 2.9) is a first step in that direction, and it pins down the right type of scaling needed.
title: Scaling limit of dynamic risk measures with Wasserstein-type one-step penalties
background_latex:
Let \(\Omega=C([0,1];\mathbb R)\) be the continuous path space with canonical process \(X_t(\omega)=\omega_t\). For \(N\in\mathbb N\), let \((Q)^N\in\mathcal P(\mathbb R^N)\) denote the pushforward of \(Q\in\mathcal P(\Omega)\) under the sampling map \(\omega\mapsto (\omega_{1/N},\dots,\omega_{N/N})\).

Fix \(p>0\). For \(Q,P\in\mathcal P_1(\mathbb R)\), define the one-step discrepancy \(D_{1,W}^{p}(Q\|\!\|P):=W_1(Q,P)^p\), where \(W_1\) is the \(1\)-Wasserstein distance on \(\mathbb R\) with cost \(|x-y|\).

For \(N>1\) and \(Q,P\in\mathcal P_1(\mathbb R^N)\) with conditional laws \(Q_i^{x_{1:i-1}}:=\mathrm{Law}_Q(X_i\mid X_{1:i-1}=x_{1:i-1})\) and \(P_i^{x_{1:i-1}}\) well-defined \(Q\)-a.s., define the time-consistent multistep discrepancy recursively by
\[
D_{N,W}^{p}(Q\|\!\|P):=D_{N-1,W}^{p}(Q_{1:N-1}\|\!\|P_{1:N-1})+\int W_1\bigl(Q_N^{x_{1:N-1}},\,P_N^{x_{1:N-1}}\bigr)^p\,dQ_{1:N-1}(x_{1:N-1}).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
D_{N,W}^{p}(Q\|\!\|P)=\int_{\mathbb R^N}\sum_{i=1}^N W_1\bigl(Q_i^{x_{1:i-1}},\,P_i^{x_{1:i-1}}\bigr)^p\,dQ(x).
\]

A classical way to build dynamic (convex) risk measures is: given a one-step penalty \(\alpha_1\) on \(\mathcal P(\mathbb R)\), define an \(N\)-step risk measure \(\rho_N\) on bounded continuous functions on \(\mathbb R^N\) via iterated one-step evaluations; its dual representation has an \(N\)-step penalty \(\alpha_N(Q)=\int \sum_{i=1}^N \alpha_1(Q_i^{x_{1:i-1}})\,dQ(x)\). In particular, taking \(\alpha_1(q)=\pm W_1(q,P)^p\) (for a chosen reference one-step law \(P\)) yields penalties expressed via \(D_{N,W}^p\).

Consider, for each \(N\), the minimax expression (a discrete-time stochastic optimization / risk-measure evaluation)
\[
\inf_H \rho_N\bigl(F_N(X)-(H\cdot X)_N\bigr)=\sup\Bigl\{\int F_N\,dQ-\alpha_N(Q):\; Q\in\mathcal P(\mathbb R^N)\text{ such that }X\text{ is a }Q\text{-martingale}\Bigr\},
\]
where \(H\) ranges over predictable trading strategies and \((H\cdot X)_N\) is the discrete-time stochastic integral against the coordinate process \(X\). One is interested in a scaling limit as the time mesh goes to \(0\), typically with \(F_N\) scaled proportionally to \(N\) (e.g. \(F_N\) depends on a continuous interpolation of the discrete path).
problem_definition_latex:
Find an appropriate universal scaling (in \(N\)) and limiting continuous-time variational problem for the quantities
\[
\frac{1}{a_N}\,\inf_H \rho_N\bigl(F_N(X)-(H\cdot X)_N\bigr)
\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
\frac{1}{a_N}\,\sup\Bigl\{\int F_N\,dQ-\alpha_N(Q):\; Q\text{ is a martingale law on }\mathbb R^N\Bigr\},
\]
when the one-step penalty is \(\alpha_1(q)=\pm W_1(q,P)^p\) (hence \(\alpha_N\) is induced by the time-consistent recursion above), and the time grid becomes fine (\(N\to\infty\)). In particular, determine whether there is a well-posed continuous-time limit on \(\Omega=C([0,1];\mathbb R)\) analogous to known scaling limits for entropy-based penalties, and identify the correct normalization \(a_N\) and limit functional when it exists.
latex:
Let \(\Omega=C([0,1];\mathbb R)\) be the continuous path space with canonical process \(X_t(\omega)=\omega_t\). For \(N\in\mathbb N\), let \((Q)^N\in\mathcal P(\mathbb R^N)\) denote the pushforward of \(Q\in\mathcal P(\Omega)\) under the sampling map \(\omega\mapsto (\omega_{1/N},\dots,\omega_{N/N})\).

Fix \(p>0\). For \(Q,P\in\mathcal P_1(\mathbb R)\), define the one-step discrepancy \(D_{1,W}^{p}(Q\|\!\|P):=W_1(Q,P)^p\), where \(W_1\) is the \(1\)-Wasserstein distance on \(\mathbb R\) with cost \(|x-y|\).

For \(N>1\) and \(Q,P\in\mathcal P_1(\mathbb R^N)\) with conditional laws \(Q_i^{x_{1:i-1}}:=\mathrm{Law}_Q(X_i\mid X_{1:i-1}=x_{1:i-1})\) and \(P_i^{x_{1:i-1}}\) well-defined \(Q\)-a.s., define the time-consistent multistep discrepancy recursively by
\[
D_{N,W}^{p}(Q\|\!\|P):=D_{N-1,W}^{p}(Q_{1:N-1}\|\!\|P_{1:N-1})+\int W_1\bigl(Q_N^{x_{1:N-1}},\,P_N^{x_{1:N-1}}\bigr)^p\,dQ_{1:N-1}(x_{1:N-1}).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
D_{N,W}^{p}(Q\|\!\|P)=\int_{\mathbb R^N}\sum_{i=1}^N W_1\bigl(Q_i^{x_{1:i-1}},\,P_i^{x_{1:i-1}}\bigr)^p\,dQ(x).

A classical way to build dynamic (convex) risk measures is: given a one-step penalty \(\alpha_1\) on \(\mathcal P(\mathbb R)\), define an \(N\)-step risk measure \(\rho_N\) on bounded continuous functions on \(\mathbb R^N\) via iterated one-step evaluations; its dual representation has an \(N\)-step penalty \(\alpha_N(Q)=\int \sum_{i=1}^N \alpha_1(Q_i^{x_{1:i-1}})\,dQ(x)\). In particular, taking \(\alpha_1(q)=\pm W_1(q,P)^p\) (for a chosen reference one-step law \(P\)) yields penalties expressed via \(D_{N,W}^p\).

Consider, for each \(N\), the minimax expression (a discrete-time stochastic optimization / risk-measure evaluation)
\[
\inf_H \rho_N\bigl(F_N(X)-(H\cdot X)_N\bigr)=\sup\Bigl\{\int F_N\,dQ-\alpha_N(Q):\; Q\in\mathcal P(\mathbb R^N)\text{ such that }X\text{ is a }Q\text{-martingale}\Bigr\},
\]
where \(H\) ranges over predictable trading strategies and \((H\cdot X)_N\) is the discrete-time stochastic integral against the coordinate process \(X\). One is interested in a scaling limit as the time mesh goes to \(0\), typically with \(F_N\) scaled proportionally to \(N\) (e.g. \(F_N\) depends on a continuous interpolation of the discrete path).

Find an appropriate universal scaling (in \(N\)) and limiting continuous-time variational problem for the quantities
\[
\frac{1}{a_N}\,\inf_H \rho_N\bigl(F_N(X)-(H\cdot X)_N\bigr)
\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
\frac{1}{a_N}\,\sup\Bigl\{\int F_N\,dQ-\alpha_N(Q):\; Q\text{ is a martingale law on }\mathbb R^N\Bigr\},
\]
when the one-step penalty is \(\alpha_1(q)=\pm W_1(q,P)^p\) (hence \(\alpha_N\) is induced by the time-consistent recursion above), and the time grid becomes fine (\(N\to\infty\)). In particular, determine whether there is a well-posed continuous-time limit on \(\Omega=C([0,1];\mathbb R)\) analogous to known scaling limits for entropy-based penalties, and identify the correct normalization \(a_N\) and limit functional when it exists.
location_in_paper: Page 8, Remark 2.4 (end of the remark, following equation (2.1))
open_problem_quote: We consider the scaling limit of (2.1) for our choice \(\alpha_1(\cdot)=\pm W_1(\cdot, P)^p\) as an interesting open question which we do not tackle here. Our main result (Theorem 2.9) is a first step in that direction, and it pins down the right type of scaling needed.
subject_classification: Scaling limits of dynamic risk measures and stochastic control under Wasserstein penalties
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a general scaling-limit theory and identification of a continuous-time limit functional, not a finite-dimensional inequality that could be refuted by searching over small instances. There is no clearly bounded, discrete search space whose exhaustive exploration would meaningfully produce counterexamples.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: While the discrete-time objective can be computed for fixed small \(N\) via optimization, the open problem is to characterize the asymptotic \(N\to\infty\) limit and correct scaling in broad generality. There is no direct automatic fitness signal for candidate theories, and verifying a proposed limit would require substantial analytical proof rather than iterative code-based evaluation.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
